title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: robSynth generates synthetic microdata presence contaminated training data. Standard sequential synthesisers synthpop (Nowok, Raab, Dibben 2016) simPop (Templ et al. 2017) use OLS CART model variable; training data contains outliers, coding errors, linkage mismatches, conditional model distorted distortion compounds subsequent variables. robSynth replaces models MM-estimation (Yohai 1987; Maronna et al. 2019) continuous variables Huber-weighted logistic regression categorical variables, extends toolbox robust tree-based methods (median-leaf CART, OOB-residual-weighted random forest, pseudo-Huber XGBoost). package supports per-variable methods, custom predictor matrices, survey weights, hierarchical (household) data, multiple synthesis Reiter’s partial-synthetic combining rules (Reiter 2003), robust Box-Cox transformations (Box Cox 1964; Marazzi Yohai 2006), utility diagnostics (pMSE, KS, regression preservation, correlation-Frobenius distance) disclosure-risk assessment (k-anonymity, closest-record distance, TCAP, RAPID (Thees, Mueller, Templ 2025)). vignette walks statistical officer applied methodologist package end--end.

Section 2 describes methods key formulae. Sections 3 4 cover installation worked contamination example. Sections 5–11 walk practitioner-oriented features (output structure, method choice, per-variable methods, transformations, formulas, predictor matrix, sentinel-coded missings). Sections 12–19 cover inference diagnostics (multiple synthesis, utility, comparison, disclosure risk, hierarchical data, survey weights, parallelisation). Sections 20–21 discuss fit-failure diagnosis migration synthpop. Section 22 summarises.

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: build small synthetic population 500 records four continuous variables two binary covariates. Unlike typical “independent normals” textbook example, X1,X2,X3X_1, X_2, X_3 drawn jointly correlation structure looks like something might see survey — X1,X2X_1, X_2 strongly correlated (ρ=0.5\rho = 0.5), X2,X3X_2, X_3 moderately (ρ=0.4\rho = 0.4), X1,X3X_1, X_3 mildly (ρ=0.3\rho = 0.3). X4X_4 linear function three continuous predictors additive Gaussian noise; factors G1,G2G_1, G_2 logistic X1X_1 X2X_2 respectively, also correlated continuous variables. enough structure make Figures 1–3 Section 15 tell non-trivial story.
code: library(robSynth)  set.seed(42) n <- 500  # Correlation structure for (X1, X2, X3) Sigma <- matrix(c(1.0, 0.5, 0.3,                   0.5, 1.0, 0.4,                   0.3, 0.4, 1.0), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) X123  <- MASS::mvrnorm(n, mu = c(0, 0, 0), Sigma = Sigma) df    <- data.frame(X1 = X123[, 1], X2 = X123[, 2], X3 = X123[, 3])  # True relationship: X4 = 0.5*X1 + 0.5*X2 + 0.3*X3 + noise df$X4 <- 0.5 * df$X1 + 0.5 * df$X2 + 0.3 * df$X3 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) df$G1 <- factor(rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * df$X1))) df$G2 <- factor(rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * df$X2)))

Section 4a repeats exercise gentler cellwise pattern closer real surveys. Note factor-flip idiom: capture original two-level set levels(df$G1) re-factor levels = ... avoid silently re-coding factor. kind subtle bug contamination-injection code tends introduce.
code: # Store clean version BEFORE contaminating (so factor levels are preserved) df_clean <- df  # 15 % contamination: +8 on X1 and X2; flip G1 on the same rows idx <- sample(n, 75) df$X1[idx] <- df$X1[idx] + 8 df$X2[idx] <- df$X2[idx] + 8 g1_lev  <- levels(df$G1) flipped <- ifelse(df$G1[idx] == g1_lev[1L], g1_lev[2L], g1_lev[1L]) df$G1[idx] <- factor(flipped, levels = g1_lev)

what: 4.3 Robust vs non-robust synthesis, averaged over 5 replications
title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: now generate five synthetic copies method, fit regression copy, record two utility numbers per replication: root-mean-square error recovered regression coefficients known truth (RMSE-beta), Frobenius norm gap original-clean synthetic correlation matrices (Correlation-Frob.). Five replications enough see pattern without making vignette slow knit; real benchmark use hundreds. Table 1: Mean utility 5 synthetic replications. Reading table. RMSE-β\beta, MM preserves true regression coefficients X4X_4 {X1,X2,X3}\{X_1, X_2, X_3\} much better OLS: contaminated rows pull OLS slopes toward shifted cloud (classical attenuation bias — least-squares fit tries accommodate bulk shifted block ), MM’s bounded ρ\rho-function limits contaminated row’s contribution objective. correlation-Frobenius distance ranking muted, metric averages 16 pairwise entries 4×44 \times 4 correlation matrix, (X1,X2)(X_1, X_2)-related entries affected. Take-away: right utility metric depends synthetic data. downstream analysis regression, RMSE-β\beta right target; correlation-based exploratory analysis, Frobenius gap . Section 22 returns population-level story; single-replication ranking population claim.
code: # 5 replications (keeps the vignette knit under ~10 s) true_coef <- c(X1 = 0.5, X2 = 0.5, X3 = 0.3) R <- 5 rmse_mm <- rmse_ols <- cor_mm <- cor_ols <- numeric(R) for (r in seq_len(R)) {   s_mm  <- robsynth(df, seed = r)$synth                             # robust default   s_ols <- robsynth(df, method = "parametric", seed = r)$synth      # OLS / plain logistic   fit_mm  <- coef(lm(X4 ~ X1 + X2 + X3, data = s_mm))[c("X1","X2","X3")]   fit_ols <- coef(lm(X4 ~ X1 + X2 + X3, data = s_ols))[c("X1","X2","X3")]   rmse_mm[r]  <- sqrt(mean((fit_mm  - true_coef)^2))   rmse_ols[r] <- sqrt(mean((fit_ols - true_coef)^2))   cor_mm[r]   <- sqrt(sum((cor(df_clean[, 1:4]) - cor(s_mm[, 1:4]))^2))   cor_ols[r]  <- sqrt(sum((cor(df_clean[, 1:4]) - cor(s_ols[, 1:4]))^2)) }  tab <- data.frame(   Method              = c("MM (robust)", "OLS (parametric)"),   `RMSE-beta`         = c(mean(rmse_mm), mean(rmse_ols)),   `Correlation-Frob.` = c(mean(cor_mm),  mean(cor_ols)),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(tab, digits = 3,              caption = "Table 1: Mean utility over 5 synthetic replications.")

MM bounds influence yy-outliers, X4X_4 (response conditional regression) cellwise-shifted values, MM downweights fitted slopes stay near truth. downstream evaluation regression lm(X4 ~ X1 + X2 + X3, data = synth) recovers true coefficients, OLS synthesis — robust fitting step — . predictor-side cellwise harder. Contamination predictor X1X_1 different problem. first variable visit sequence drawn marginally empirical distribution (weighted resample + kernel bandwidth), marginal draw robust cellwise outliers variable — 10 % shifted X1X_1 cells get sampled synthetic X1X_1. MM subsequent conditional models handles response side, contamination X1X_1 remains propagates. want full cellwise protection across variables, use method = "robust_rf" method = "robust_xgboost" (downweight outliers inside tree fits) pre-process original data cellwise filter (e.g. DDC, cellMCD) calling robsynth(). companion paper [Templ 2026, submitted] discusses trade-offs; full cellwise preprocessing integrated robsynth() planned future release.
code: set.seed(11) df_cw <- df_clean cell_prob <- 0.10 flip <- runif(nrow(df_cw)) < cell_prob df_cw$X4[flip] <- df_cw$X4[flip] + 15   # response-side outliers  R_cw <- 5 rmse_mm_cw <- rmse_ols_cw <- numeric(R_cw) for (r in seq_len(R_cw)) {   s_mm_cw  <- robsynth(df_cw, seed = r)$synth   s_ols_cw <- robsynth(df_cw, method = "parametric", seed = r)$synth   rmse_mm_cw[r]  <- sqrt(mean((coef(lm(X4 ~ X1 + X2 + X3, data = s_mm_cw ))[2:4]                                - true_coef)^2))   rmse_ols_cw[r] <- sqrt(mean((coef(lm(X4 ~ X1 + X2 + X3, data = s_ols_cw))[2:4]                                - true_coef)^2)) }  tab_cw <- data.frame(   Method      = c("MM (robust)", "OLS (parametric)"),   `RMSE-beta` = c(mean(rmse_mm_cw), mean(rmse_ols_cw)),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(tab_cw, digits = 3,              caption = "Table 1b: Cellwise response contamination (10 %, shift = 15; mean over 5 replications).")

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Calling robsynth(df) returns list--class "robsynth" bundling synthetic data together configuration fitted conditional models. print() method summarises object without dumping full synthetic data frame. almost every downstream use need $synth. components kept auditing, reproducibility, diagnostics: $synth — synthetic data frame (list data frames m > 1). Factor levels match original, NAs cont.na propagated, can write straight CSV write.csv(res$synth, ...). $original — training data stored verbatim. Convenient diagnostic functions like utility(res) pull automatically; remove strip_diagnostics(res) releasing synthetic copy external users, effectively releasing training data along . $m, $method, $visit.sequence, $predictor.matrix, $transform_info — configuration used synthesis, exactly resolved call time. Useful self-documenting audit trail. $models — fitted conditional models last synthetic copy. str(res$models, max.level = 1) gives quick overview. Inspect synthetic column looks suspicious (Section 20 convergence failures); release encode information original data. $var_types — named character vector "continuous" "categorical" per column. Useful downstream scripts need handle two types differently. practice idiom :
code: res <- robsynth(df, seed = 1) print(res) #> robSynth result #>   Variables:  6 ( 4 continuous, 2 categorical ) #>   Synthetic n: 500  #>   Methods: mm, robust_logreg res <- robsynth(df, seed = 1) write.csv(res$synth, "synthetic_release.csv", row.names = FALSE) save(strip_diagnostics(res), file = "synthetic_release.rda")

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: defaults (mm continuous, robust_logreg categorical) work well typical “messy official-statistics” case; ten methods exist specific problems. Use following rules thumb starting point refine based utility() diagnostics: Clean data, linear structure → norm (parametric, fastest) cart (captures non-linearities). confident contamination, reason pay overhead robust estimators. Contaminated continuous linear structure → mm (default). sweet spot: linear form correctly specified robustness buys protection outliers. Contaminated continuous, non-linear → robust_rf (two-pass random forest OOB-residual weights) robust_xgboost (pseudo-Huber loss). Use robust_rf interpretability matters, robust_xgboost need push predictive performance willing pay fit cost. Heavy-tailed skewed continuous → mm combined transform = list(var = "boxcox") (Section 8). robust Box-Cox lambda estimator (robust_boxcox_lambda) resistant outliers motivated transformation. Binary multinomial rare classes → robust_logreg (default). clean multi-level data without concern contamination, polyreg faster. Strictly derived variable (e.g. BMI=weight/height2BMI = \text{weight} / \text{height}^2, total income sum components) → passive formulas entry (Section 9). Never synthesise derived quantity directly; synthesise components re-derive. Variables must kept verbatim (e.g. geographic region, year--birth epidemiology cohorts) → sample (marginal resampling) variables; leaves variable identical bootstrap original values. doubt, run robsynth() default inspect utility(res) clean data (Section 13). pMSE much larger theoretical null c(1−c)/nc(1-c)/n, try robust_rf robust_xgboost — large pMSE typically flags missed non-linearity rather contamination issue. can also mix methods across variables (Section 7), right move whenever different columns different problems.

Anything leave unnamed gets corresponding default: continuous columns fall back mm, categorical columns robust_logreg. lets override variables opinion . table summarises backends optional package dependencies. robust variants bold ones typically want contamination; non-robust classics kept compatibility synthpop / simPop pipelines. Packages listed Requires Suggests; install demand corresponding method. package missing, robsynth() errors informative message rather silently falling back.

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Many real variables (income, reaction times, counts, distances) heavily skewed. MM-estimation assumes approximately symmetric conditional errors, fitting mm directly income variable systematically mis-fit right tail. standard remedy Box-Cox transform (Box Cox 1964) synthesis inverse transform synthetic output. classical Box-Cox lambda estimated maximum likelihood, sensitive outliers — exactly situation trying robustify . robust_boxcox_lambda() minimises robust skewness proxy, |mean−median|/MAD|\text{mean}-\text{median}|/\text{MAD}, user-specified grid (Marazzi Yohai 2006). example add income-like variable via rlnorm(), inflate already-contaminated rows tenfold create heavy tail, let transform = list(income = "boxcox") pick lambda automatically. lambda near 0 corresponds log transform, expected answer log-normal input. Supported transforms "log" "sqrt" (fixed), "boxcox" (estimated). applied original column fitting conditional model, inverse applied synthesis — synthetic income column original scale. shift used guarantee strict positivity recorded res3$transform_info downstream consumers can reverse transform manually needed.
code: set.seed(123) df$income <- rlnorm(n, 10, 0.5) df$income[idx] <- df$income[idx] * 10                      # contaminate  # Robust Box-Cox normalises before synthesis, back-transforms after res3 <- robsynth(df, transform = list(income = "boxcox"), seed = 1) cat("Estimated lambda:", res3$transform_info$income$lambda, "\n") #> Estimated lambda: -0.8 # Lambda near 0 = log-transform (expected for log-normal data)

text: visit.sequence controls order variables synthesised. variable conditioned variables come sequence. default visit order matches order columns data, places categorical covariates continuous variables — sometimes want opposite (e.g. synthesise demographic factors first continuous variables can condition ). formulas overrides default “right-hand side = prior variables” rule individual variables. example , X3 = ~ X1 * X2 + G1 entry tells synthesiser condition X3X_3 interaction X1X_1 X2X_2 plus factor G1G_1, instead default additive conditioning prior variables. cleanest way inject interactions, polynomials, passive-style relationships. variables mentioned formulas get custom formula; every variable keeps default. analysis model many interactions, specify rather trying encode via predictor matrix Section 10 — formula expressive matrix binary.
code: res4 <- robsynth(df,   visit.sequence = c("G1", "G2", "X1", "X2", "X3", "X4", "income"),   formulas = list(X3 = ~ X1 * X2 + G1),   transform = list(income = "log"),   seed = 1)

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Many NSO datasets encode “refused”, “don’t know”, “applicable” numeric sentinels (-9, -999, 9999, ., .B, …) rather NA. feed values synthesiser without recoding, two bad things happen: Variable-type detection may flag column “categorical” concentration sentinel values, estimate MAD distribution includes -999 points, turning column’s scale estimate nonsense. sentinel value reappears synthetic output — downstream users assume valid observation. cont.na argument recodes sentinel values NA type detection scale estimator invoked, timing lets rest pipeline treat sentinels correctly. also equivalent cont.na argument synthpop. cont.na accepts named list whose entries map column names either single sentinel value vector (list(income = c(-9, -99, -999))). numeric columns affected; factor columns recoded NA directly calling robsynth().
code: df_na <- df df_na$income[1:20] <- -9                                   # "refused" res_na <- robsynth(df_na, cont.na = list(income = -9), seed = 1) # The sentinel does not reappear in the synthetic output any(res_na$synth$income == -9, na.rm = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE

code: res5 <- robsynth(df[, 1:6], m = 5, seed = 1) fit  <- synth_lm(X4 ~ X1 + X2 + X3, res5) pooled <- data.frame(   term = names(fit$coefficients),   coef = round(fit$coefficients, 4),   se   = round(fit$se,           4),   df   = round(fit$df,           1) ) knitr::kable(pooled, row.names = FALSE,              caption = "Table 2: Reiter (2003) partially-synthetic combined estimates (m = 5).")

text: Important — contamination paradox. utility() compares synthetic data stored $original, contaminated training data. successful robust synthesiser produces synthetic data closer clean distribution training data , contaminated baseline can look “worse” — regression coefficients right place, contaminated OLS baseline’s coefficients , CIs overlap. Always compute utility clean reference one available. Table 3: Utility contaminated training data vs clean truth. CI-overlap collapses zero measured contamination synthesised coefficients sit near TRUE values OLS contaminated data . pMSE reported raw scale. null perfect synthesis expected value c(1−c)/nc(1-c)/n c=nsynth/(norig+nsynth)c = n_\mathrm{synth}/(n_\mathrm{orig} + n_\mathrm{synth}) nn total sample size; equal-size synthetic copy null 1/(4n)1/(4n). cross-sample-size comparison, Snoke Slavković (2018) recommend null-scaled pMSE/Var(pMSE∣H0)\mathrm{pMSE} / \mathrm{Var}(\mathrm{pMSE} \mid H_0). Values near 1 indistinguishable perfect synthesiser; larger values indicate classifier can tell original synthetic records apart. Heuristic (prescriptive) diagnostic guides clean-baseline utility: Mean KS < 0.05: marginals broadly preserved. Correlation-Frobenius < 0.1 (divided number continuous variables): joint linear structure broadly preserved. CI overlap near 1: inferential conclusions broadly preserved. Compared contaminated baseline, three can look much worse even synthetic data objectively closer truth. practical recipe: clean reference available (e.g. pre-contamination data, validation pilot, well-curated subset), run utility(res$synth, clean_data) measure well synthesiser recovers target distribution. contaminated training data available, treat utility metrics diagnostic absolute. Numbers look “bad” may simply reflect synthesiser right thing left contamination behind. Always pair utility disclosure-risk check (Section 16). high-utility synthetic dataset matches original record record useless — original dataset.

The CI-overlap collapses to zero when measured against contamination because the synthesised coefficients sit near the TRUE values while OLS on the contaminated data does not.")

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Aggregate numbers necessary never sufficient — good synthesiser can excellent pMSE still miss tails bimodal distribution get sign correlation wrong. visual diagnostics cover three complementary perspectives: univariate marginals, bivariate correlation structure, pairwise joint distribution. three use Okabe-Ito palette colourblind safety (original = black / grey, synthetic = orange). Figure 1 — marginals. compare() draws overlaid densities continuous variables grouped bar plots factors. continuous facet label shows Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic — crude useful scalar summary close two marginals . look : matched modes, matched tails, systematic shift. Figure 1: Marginal distributions category proportions — original (black) vs synthetic (orange). KS statistic shown facet label. Figure 2 — correlation structure, paradox. natural default compare two heatmaps: classical Pearson correlation original data, classical Pearson correlation synthetic. contamination classical original biased — Pearson correlation breakdown point zero, 15 % block shift can drive -diagonal entries anywhere. robust synthesiser recovered clean correlation structure, classical panel differ classical-original panel design. Reading two-panel comparison “agree” punishes successful robust synthesis — paradox CI-overlap collapse Section 13. 2×2 grid lets reader separate two independent questions. Rows = correlation measured (top = classical Pearson, bottom = OGK-based robust, Maronna et al. (2019)); columns = dataset (left = original, right = synthetic). Reading grid: Top row shows classical analyst see. Top-left biased contamination; top-right synthetic Pearson correlation — can match either contaminated clean truth depending whether synthesiser left contamination . Bottom row best guess clean correlation look like. Bottom-left bottom-right agree top-right synthesiser job; top-left meant discrepant. Columns (original vs synthetic within row) show much dataset-construction step changed correlation measured consistently. Pass include_robust_original = TRUE plot(..., type = "correlation") get 2×2 rendering diagnostics; pass cor_method = "robust" alone get two-panel robust-vs-robust comparison. Figure 2: Correlation structure — 2x2 grid heatmaps. Rows: classical Pearson (top) vs OGK-robust (bottom). Columns: original (left) vs synthetic (right). top-left panel biased contamination; remaining three panels agree robust synthesiser recovered clean structure. Figure 3 — pairwise joint distribution. Scatter plots first three continuous variables reveal whether synthesiser captures joint structure, just marginals. Two variables can identical marginals different joint behaviour (correlation, tail dependence, holes support), plot diagnostic way Figure 1 . Pairwise scatter comes two layouts: "overlay" (default) puts sources shared axis transparency, "facet" places two side--side panels. Overlay faster read two clouds differ location: immediately see orange synthetic points sitting grey original points (). Facet clearer clouds overlap heavily shape differs — can see holes density asymmetries overlay smooths . alpha argument controls point transparency; default (0.4 overlay, 0.6 facet) usually fine. Figure 3a: Pairwise scatter, overlay layout — original (grey) synthetic (orange) shared axis; alpha = 0.4. Figure 3b: Pairwise scatter, facet layout — original (left panel) synthetic (right panel) separate sub-plots; alpha = 0.6.

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Household surveys, education cohorts, longitudinal panels — many real datasets come two-level form records within cluster exchangeable records clusters. Naively synthesising data record level destroys cluster structure (e.g. producing “households” whose members’ incomes contradict household total). robsynth_hh() handles two-phase procedure: Phase 1 resamples whole households replacement. synthetic household keeps member-count household-level variables (income, region, size) intact — synthesised, bootstrapped. Phase 2 synthesises person-level variables (age, sex, etc.) conditional household-level variables previously synthesised person variables. conditional models fit original full data predicted resampled household structure, means person-level distributions reflect full empirical population, just bootstrapped households.
code: set.seed(1) hh <- data.frame(   hhid      = rep(1:100, each = 3),   hh_income = rep(rnorm(100, 5000, 1000), each = 3),   region    = factor(rep(sample(c("A", "B"), 100, TRUE), each = 3)),   age       = rnorm(300, 40, 15),   sex       = factor(sample(c("M", "F"), 300, TRUE)) ) hh$hh_income[1:15] <- hh$hh_income[1:15] + 8000            # hh outliers  res_hh <- suppressMessages(   robsynth_hh(hh, hhid = "hhid",               hh_vars     = c("hh_income", "region"),               person_vars = c("age", "sex"),               seed = 1) ) print(res_hh) #> robSynth hierarchical result #>   Households: 100  #>   Persons: 300  #>   HH vars: hh_income, region  #>   Person vars: age, sex all(table(res_hh$synth$hhid) == 3)                         # sizes preserved #> [1] TRUE

text: Warning. NULL entry res$models signals silent fit failure: continuous path fell back lm() categorical path fell back marginal sampling. Always inspect sapply(res$models, .null) release, re-run alternative method variable whose model NULL. Occasionally lmrob fails converge (severe singular design, fewer rows predictors NA omission, factor singleton level contamination). default continuous path silently falls back lm() case; categorical paths stored model NULL generator falls back marginal sampling — .e. variable drawn empirical marginal distribution, ignoring conditioning. Silent fallback deliberate: hard error middle sequential synthesis leave partially-populated synthetic dataset. mean need check. Practical checks: Remedies common failure modes: Factor singleton class contamination — collapse rare classes synthesis (droplevels() followed forcats::fct_lump_min(min = 2)), explicitly use method = "sample" variable. robust_xgboost unseen factor levels new data — apply droplevels() training data first training xlev exhaustive. Multicollinearity singular design — inspect predictor matrix (Section 10); drop one collinear predictors row problematic variable. small sample (n<2pn < 2p) — try method = "cart" method = "sample" affected variables; MM needs enough degrees freedom estimate scale.

title: robSynth: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: robSynth plugs sequential-synthesis idiom synthpop simPop replaces every OLS/CART conditional bounded-influence alternative. headline guarantees — 50 % breakdown MM, conditional y-outlier flagging via OOB residuals robust_rf, pseudo-Huber loss robust_xgboost, Reiter’s partial combining m>1m>1 inference — summarised Section 2 demonstrated Sections 4 onward. package designed practitioners: per-variable methods, sentinel-code recoding, survey weights, hierarchical data, six riskutility disclosure-risk measures (RAPID, DCAP, DCR, δ\delta-presence, DOMIAS, drisk) first-class citizens. Section 13 highlights “contamination paradox”: utility metrics computed contaminated training data penalise successful robust synthesis. simulation study accompanying paper [Templ 2026, submitted] quantifies population-level story block cellwise contamination; ranking methods single demo interpreted illustrative rather definitive.

title: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Robust Synthetic Data Generation – protecting data quality contaminated training data. Real-world microdata often contain outliers, coding errors, measurement artifacts. Standard synthesis methods (OLS, CART) propagate errors synthetic output, degrading utility inflating disclosure risk. robSynth replaces conditional models sequential synthesis MM-estimators continuous variables (50% breakdown point, 95% asymptotic efficiency) Huber-weighted logistic regression categorical variables. result synthetic data whose statistical properties close clean data-generating process, even training data contaminated.

title: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: Robust MM synthesis – MM-estimation via robustbase::lmrob continuous variables; weighted multinomial logit categorical variables. Contamination detection – optional cellwise contamination filtering (method = "contam_filter") downweights suspicious cells synthesis. Utility metrics – propensity score MSE (pMSE), Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics, regression coefficient preservation, correlation matrix distance, empirical distribution overlap. Risk metrics – k-anonymity, closest-record distance, membership inference AUC, Target Correct Attribution Probability (TCAP). Risk-utility frontier – risk_utility_frontier() compares multiple synthesizers Duncan-Lambert R-U confidentiality map identifies Pareto-optimal methods.

title: Robust Synthetic Data Generation
text: robSynth adds robustness sequential synthesis framework popularized synthpop. contamination, MM-based synthesis reduces pMSE 44–59% compared non-robust methods (OLS, CART), maintaining comparable disclosure risk levels.

title: Disclosure risk assessment — disclosure
text: Computes one disclosure-risk measures synthetic data. riskutility package installed following measures available: "rapid" Risk Attribute Prediction-Induced Disclosure     (Thees, Mueller & Templ, 2025): trains predictive attacker     synthetic data scores success original. "dcap" Differential Correct Attribution Probability —     exact- Gower-matching attack key variables sensitive     attribute recovery. "dcr" Distance Closest Record — per-record nearest     distance holdout null. "delta_presence" \(\delta\)-presence: proportion     original records whose inclusion can inferred. "domias" Density-based membership inference attack:     AUC train-vs-holdout separation density ratio. "drisk" sdcMicro-style disclosure risk     key-vars (interval / robust-Mahalanobis). built-"distance" measure simple robust-MAD nearest- neighbour distance require riskutility.  special value "" expands every available measure whose dependencies satisfied.

title: Estimate contamination model — estimate_contamination
text: data data.frame method "cellwise" (default) estimates per-cell posterior clean probabilities; "rowwise" uses MCD-based Mahalanobis distances flag entire rows. eps_init initial contamination probability per variable. Default: 0.1. Must \((0, 0.5)\). gamma_init initial scale inflation factor contamination distribution. Default: 3. maxit maximum EM iterations. Default: 100. tol convergence tolerance relative parameter change. Default: 1e-4. alpha fraction data MCD (rowwise ). Default: 0.75.

title: Estimate contamination model — estimate_contamination
text: list components: clean_probs n x p matrix posterior clean probabilities     (values \([0, 1]\)). Categorical columns weight 1. epsilon named numeric vector estimated per-variable     contamination rates (continuous ) method method used converged logical iterations number EM iterations performed

title: Estimate contamination model — estimate_contamination
text: Cellwise method. continuous variable \(j\), observed value modeled two-component mixture: $$(1 - \varepsilon_j) \cdot N(\mu_j, \sigma_j^2) +   \varepsilon_j \cdot N(\mu_j, (\gamma_j \sigma_j)^2)$$ EM algorithm iterates computing posterior clean probabilities (E-step) updating parameters (M-step). Important (Issue 20): Cellwise z-scores use \(z_j = (x - \mathrm{med}) / \mathrm{mad}\), \(|x - \mathrm{med}| / \mathrm{mad}\). absolute value folds distribution makes dnorm(z_j) incorrect. Rowwise method. Uses robust Mahalanobis distances robustbase::covMcd converts row-level clean probabilities via chi-squared tail probability.

title: Estimate contamination model — estimate_contamination
text: Matthias Templ

text: Maintainer: Matthias Templ matthias.templ@fhnw.ch (ORCID)

title: Robust synthetic data generation — robsynth
text: Main entry point robSynth package.  Generates synthetic data (possibly contaminated) original dataset using robust sequential conditional synthesis.

title: Robust synthetic data generation — robsynth
code: robsynth(   data,   method = NULL,   m = 1L,   visit.sequence = NULL,   predictor.matrix = NULL,   formulas = NULL,   transform = NULL,   weights = NULL,   n_synth = nrow(data),   seed = NULL,   proper = FALSE,   cont.na = NULL,   n_cores = 1L,   ... )

title: Robust synthetic data generation — robsynth
text: data data.frame (data.table, converted internally) method synthesis method(s).  Either single string applied variables, named character vector one method per variable. Available methods continuous variables: "mm" (MM-estimation, default robust), "norm" (OLS + normal noise), "cart" (CART via rpart), "ctree" (conditional inference tree), "sample" (marginal resampling). categorical variables: "robust_logreg" (Huber-weighted logistic, default robust), "logreg" (standard logistic), "polyreg" (multinomial logistic via nnet), "cart", "ctree", "sample". Special methods: "passive" (deterministic function variables, see   formulas), "" (skip: variable synthesised, used predictor ). m number synthetic copies generate.  Default 1. m > 1, synth component list data frames combining rules apply model-based inference. visit.sequence character vector specifying order variables synthesised.  Default: column order data. predictor.matrix \(p \times p\) binary matrix entry [j, k] = 1 means variable k used predictor variable j.  Default: lower-triangular (variable predicted prior variables visit sequence). formulas optional named list formulas.  variables custom formulas, right-hand side specifies predictors optional interactions transformations (e.g., list(y = ~ x1 * x2 + (x3^2))).  method = "passive", formula specifies deterministic function (e.g., list(bmi = ~ (weight / height^2 * 10000))). transform optional named list mapping variable names transformation type: "log", "sqrt", "boxcox" (robust Box-Cox robustly estimated lambda).  Transformations applied synthesis back-transformed afterwards. weights survey/design weights.  Either character string naming column data (removed synthesis variables), numeric vector length nrow(data). Weights passed fitting functions (lmrob, lm, glm). n_synth number synthetic records.  Default: nrow(data). seed random seed reproducibility. proper logical.  Currently ignored (plug-synthesis ). Future versions support bootstrap-based proper synthesis. cont.na optional named list values treat NA continuous variables (e.g., list(income = c(-8, -9))). n_cores number cores parallel synthesis m > 1.  Default 1 (sequential). ... additional arguments passed fitting functions.

title: Robust synthesis for hierarchical (household/person) data — robsynth_hh
text: Phase 1 (Structure): Households resampled replacement original data.  Household-level variables (specified via hh_vars) carried resampled households. Household sizes preserved. Phase 2 (Person-level): person-level variable, robust model fitted full original data using previously synthesised variables (including household-level) predictors. Synthetic values drawn conditional household context.

title: Robust synthesis for hierarchical (household/person) data — robsynth_hh
text: Matthias Templ

title: robSynth 0.5.0
text: New methods: "robust_cart" (median-leaf CART), "robust_rf" (ranger OOB-residual-based case weights), "robust_xgboost" (pseudo-Huber loss). robust_rf two-pass estimator: preliminary forest produces OOB residuals, rows residual z-score > 3 downweighted, forest refit. robust_xgboost stores xlev training column names new-data design matrices aligned, preventing silent garbage predictions factor levels differ train predict.

title: robSynth 0.4.0
text: utility() S3 generic methods "robsynth" "data.frame": returns pMSE, KS, regression coefficient preservation, correlation-matrix distance, distribution overlap. compare_fit() compares model fitted original data model fitted synthetic data, returning coefficient standard- error differences plus Reiter-pooled CIs m > 1. disclosure() unified disclosure-risk entry point: k-anonymity, closest-record distance, membership inference AUC, TCAP, RAPID (via optional riskutility). compare() plot() S3 methods side--side marginal correlation diagnostics.

title: robSynth 0.3.0
text: weights = argument survey-weighted synthesis (accepts variable name data numeric vector). robsynth_hh() two-phase hierarchical synthesis: Phase 1 resamples households replacement; Phase 2 synthesises person-level variables using models fit original data. n_cores argument parallel synthesis across m > 1 draws via parallel::parLapply. Per-draw seeds make parallel sequential paths produce identical output seed set.

title: robSynth 0.2.0
text: Per-variable method vector via method = c(var = "mm", ...). predictor.matrix user-controlled conditioning. formulas custom formulae (including interactions passive relationships). m > 1 multiple synthesis Reiter (2003) combining rules synth_lm() synth_glm(). transform = list(var = "log" | "sqrt" | "boxcox") robust_boxcox_lambda() — robust Box-Cox minimises mean-vs-median skewness proxy rather standard MLE. cont.na argument recoding sentinel values NA synthesis (applied variable-type detection scale estimates contaminated sentinel codes). "passive" method deterministic transformations already- synthesised variables.

title: robSynth 0.1.0
text: Initial release. robsynth(): sequential synthesis MM-estimation (continuous) weighted multinomial logit (categorical). Utility metrics via synth_utility(): pMSE, KS, regression preservation, correlation matrix distance, distribution overlap. Risk metrics via synth_risk(): k-anonymity, closest-record distance, membership inference AUC, TCAP. risk_utility_frontier() comparing multiple synthesisers Duncan-Lambert R-U confidentiality map. estimate_contamination() cellwise contamination detection. strip_diagnostics() remove sensitive diagnostics data release.
